EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong winter storm brought blizzard conditions to South-Central Nebraska.  Only 4 or less inches of snow fell across the region but this snow was driven by 60 to 90 mph winds.  Temperatures fell over 30 degrees at the beginning of the event to readings near zero.  This produced wind chills of 50 to 70 below zero.  Most of the snow was over during the early morning hours of the 18th but the strong winds continued making for whiteout conditions.  Interstate 80 was closed until the 19th as it was littered with nearly 300 vehicles.  US Highway 30 was also a parking lot for about 250 abandoned vehicles.  No deaths or serious injuries were directly related to this blizzard.

Strong winds sustained from 30 to 40 mph were reported across Eastern Nebraska and Southwest Iowa with gusts from 45 to 58 mph.

An intense winter storm produced near blizzard conditions and left 3 to 10 inches of snow in its wake.  A general 5 to 6 inch band of snow fell from Lincoln and Auburn northeast to Omaha and Tekamah.  Heavier amounts of 7 to 10 inches were reported in Papillion and continued into western Iowa.  In addition to the snow, strong winds from 30 to 40 mph reduced visibilities to less than one mile for over 12 hours in Lincoln and Omaha areas.  Wind chills ranged from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  Numerous schools were closed and several businesses closed by late afternoon.
Strong winds sustained from 30 to 40 mph were reported across Eastern Nebraska and Southwest Iowa with gusts from 45 to 58 mph.

A major winter storm with sustained winds of 40 mph and gusts to 65 mph dropped 5 to 6 inches of snow over Sheridan, Cherry, Grant, Thomas and Custer counties of North Central Ne.  Three inches to trace amounts fell over Southwest and West Central Ne.  The wind combined with temperatures from 5 above to 5 below zero produced wind chills from 45 to 65 below zero and reduced visibilities to near zero on the 17th thru the 18th.  A portion of Interstaste 80 from Ogallala to York was closed from the night of the 17th thru the morning of the 19th. A 52 year old man was found frozen to death in his garage in Arnold Ne., Custer County on the 18th. Power lines whipped by the high winds caused 200k damage to a power substation in Broken Bow, Custer Co.  In Imperial, Chase County, three 21,000 gal fiberglass fertilizer storage tanks were blown over onto a pickup casusing extensive damage to the pickup and the 3 tanks.

Deep low pressure moving east across Kansas combined with Arctic high pressure diving southeast to produce blizzard conditions over North Central Nebraska.  While most areas received 3 to 5 inches of new snow, parts of Brown County reported 6 to 8 inches.  The new snow and northwest winds gusting to 60 mph created near-zero visibilities and snow drifts up to 8 feet.  Highways and local roads were impassable.  The strong winds and single digit temperatures combined to produce extreme wind chills of 45 to 60 degrees below zero.  All schools and most businesses were closed the 18th.  Numerous local power outages occurred; lasting an average of 2 to 3 hours.  Only minor livestock losses were reported, which included a few hogs in Holt County.

Winter storm winds gusting to 65 mph resulted in damage to glass displays, a glass door, a roof, and a portable livestock building in Norfolk.  Winds gusting to 70 mph demolished an $18,000 building in Hartington and destroyed grain bins in Beemer.  High winds damaged part of the Albion Elementary School addition that was under construction and did tree and shed damage in Thurston County.

Arctic high pressure dominated the weather over much of the central United States.  Sub-zero temperatures were reported at Omaha for 63 consecutive hours from early February 1st through February 4th.  While area hospitals reported treating several people for frostbite, one frostbite victim was detailed in a newspaper article.  The peak of the cold wave occurred during the morning of February 2nd with a low temperature of 23 degrees below zero at Omaha.  Many locations reported lows from 20 to 25 degrees below zero that morning.

An intense spring storm system brought beneficial rains to the area, but also a blizzard.  During the afternoon and evening of the 24th, rain changed to freezing rain and then snow.  Snowfall from 2 to 8 inches fell, with the heavier, 6 inch or greater band from near Grand Island northeast to Norfolk and Laurel.  Winds were sustained at 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero from 8 pm the 24th until 6 am the 25th.  Temperatures fell to the single digits with wind chills ranging from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  This dangerous storm stranded hundreds of motorists and resulted in numerous accidents and reports of wind damage.

A blast of Arctic air invaded eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 17th.  The day started out with dense fog and temperatures around freezing.  By late afternoon, a large temperature gradient had been established as Norfolk had switched over to freezing drizzle and Falls City was in the 60's.  A strong cold front moved through the area plummeting temperatures and was accompanied by sustained winds from 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  While light rain preceded the frontal passage, only a dusting to 1 inch of snow fell behind the cold front.  Extreme wind chills from -40 to -60 degrees persisted for 31 hours.

The peak of the cold wave occurred during the morning of February 2nd with a low temperature of 23 degrees below zero at Omaha.  Many locations reported lows from 20 to 25 degrees below zero that morning.

An intense winter storm produced near blizzard conditions and left 3 to 10 inches of snow in its wake.  A general 5 to 6 inch band of snow fell from Lincoln and Auburn northeast to Omaha and Tekamah.  Heavier amounts of 7 to 10 inches were reported in Papillion and continued into western Iowa.  In addition to the snow, strong winds from 30 to 40 mph reduced visibilities to less than one mile for over 12 hours in Lincoln and Omaha areas.  Wind chills ranged from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  Numerous schools were closed and several businesses closed by late afternoon.

A very strong winter storm brought blizzard conditions to South-Central Nebraska.  Only 4 or less inches of snow fell across the region but this snow was driven by 60 to 90 mph winds.  Temperatures fell over 30 degrees at the beginning of the event to readings near zero.  This produced wind chills of 50 to 70 below zero.  Most of the snow was over during the early morning hours of the 18th but the strong winds continued making for whiteout conditions.  Interstate 80 was closed until the 19th as it was littered with nearly 300 vehicles.  US Highway 30 was also a parking lot for about 250 abandoned vehicles.  No deaths or serious injuries were directly related to this blizzard.

A blast of Arctic air invaded eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 17th.  The day started out with dense fog and temperatures around freezing.  By late afternoon, a large temperature gradient had been established as Norfolk had switched over to freezing drizzle and Falls City was in the 60's.  A strong cold front moved through the area plummeting temperatures and was accompanied by sustained winds from 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  While light rain preceded the frontal passage, only a dusting to 1 inch of snow fell behind the cold front.  Extreme wind chills from -40 to -60 degrees persisted for 31 hours.
Strong winds sustained from 30 to 45 mph blew across the state of Nebraska with gusts from 47 to 65 mph in Eastern Nebraska and Southwest Iowa.

Winter storm winds gusting to 65 mph resulted in damage to glass displays, a glass door, a roof, and a portable livestock building in Norfolk.  Winds gusting to 70 mph demolished an $18,000 building in Hartington and destroyed grain bins in Beemer.  High winds damaged part of the Albion Elementary School addition that was under construction and did tree and shed damage in Thurston County.

An intense spring storm system brought beneficial rains to the area, but also a blizzard.  During the afternoon and evening of the 24th, rain changed to freezing rain and then snow.  Snowfall from 2 to 8 inches fell, with the heavier, 6 inch or greater band from near Grand Island northeast to Norfolk and Laurel.  Winds were sustained at 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero from 8 pm the 24th until 6 am the 25th.  Temperatures fell to the single digits with wind chills ranging from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  This dangerous storm stranded hundreds of motorists and resulted in numerous accidents and reports of wind damage.

A major winter storm with sustained winds of 40 mph and gusts to 65 mph dropped 5 to 6 inches of snow over Sheridan, Cherry, Grant, Thomas and Custer counties of North Central Ne.  Three inches to trace amounts fell over Southwest and West Central Ne.  The wind combined with temperatures from 5 above to 5 below zero produced wind chills from 45 to 65 below zero and reduced visibilities to near zero on the 17th thru the 18th.  A portion of Interstaste 80 from Ogallala to York was closed from the night of the 17th thru the morning of the 19th. A 52 year old man was found frozen to death in his garage in Arnold Ne., Custer County on the 18th. Power lines whipped by the high winds caused 200k damage to a power substation in Broken Bow, Custer Co.  In Imperial, Chase County, three 21,000 gal fiberglass fertilizer storage tanks were blown over onto a pickup casusing extensive damage to the pickup and the 3 tanks.

A very strong winter storm brought blizzard conditions to South-Central Nebraska.  Only 4 or less inches of snow fell across the region but this snow was driven by 60 to 90 mph winds.  Temperatures fell over 30 degrees at the beginning of the event to readings near zero.  This produced wind chills of 50 to 70 below zero.  Most of the snow was over during the early morning hours of the 18th but the strong winds continued making for whiteout conditions.  Interstate 80 was closed until the 19th as it was littered with nearly 300 vehicles.  US Highway 30 was also a parking lot for about 250 abandoned vehicles.  No deaths or serious injuries were directly related to this blizzard.

An intense winter storm produced near blizzard conditions and left 3 to 10 inches of snow in its wake.  A general 5 to 6 inch band of snow fell from Lincoln and Auburn northeast to Omaha and Tekamah.  Heavier amounts of 7 to 10 inches were reported in Papillion and continued into western Iowa.  In addition to the snow, strong winds from 30 to 40 mph reduced visibilities to less than one mile for over 12 hours in Lincoln and Omaha areas.  Wind chills ranged from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  Numerous schools were closed and several businesses closed by late afternoon.
Strong winds sustained from 30 to 40 mph were reported across Eastern Nebraska and Southwest Iowa with gusts from 45 to 58 mph.

A blast of Arctic air invaded eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 17th.  The day started out with dense fog and temperatures around freezing.  By late afternoon, a large temperature gradient had been established as Norfolk had switched over to freezing drizzle and Falls City was in the 60's.  A strong cold front moved through the area plummeting temperatures and was accompanied by sustained winds from 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  While light rain preceded the frontal passage, only a dusting to 1 inch of snow fell behind the cold front.  Extreme wind chills from -40 to -60 degrees persisted for 31 hours.

An intense spring storm system brought beneficial rains to the area, but also a blizzard.  During the afternoon and evening of the 24th, rain changed to freezing rain and then snow.  Snowfall from 2 to 8 inches fell, with the heavier, 6 inch or greater band from near Grand Island northeast to Norfolk and Laurel.  Winds were sustained at 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero from 8 pm the 24th until 6 am the 25th.  Temperatures fell to the single digits with wind chills ranging from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  This dangerous storm stranded hundreds of motorists and resulted in numerous accidents and reports of wind damage.
High winds and extreme cold hit the area around midday and lasted until early morning.  Damage occurred to trees, fences, power lines, and roofs.  Traffic was halted on roads.

Deep low pressure moving east across Kansas combined with Arctic high pressure diving southeast to produce blizzard conditions over North Central Nebraska.  While most areas received 3 to 5 inches of new snow, parts of Brown County reported 6 to 8 inches.  The new snow and northwest winds gusting to 60 mph created near-zero visibilities and snow drifts up to 8 feet.  Highways and local roads were impassable.  The strong winds and single digit temperatures combined to produce extreme wind chills of 45 to 60 degrees below zero.  All schools and most businesses were closed the 18th.  Numerous local power outages occurred; lasting an average of 2 to 3 hours.  Only minor livestock losses were reported, which included a few hogs in Holt County.
Strong winds were over the area.

A major winter storm with sustained winds of 40 mph and gusts to 65 mph dropped 5 to 6 inches of snow over Sheridan, Cherry, Grant, Thomas and Custer counties of North Central Ne.  Three inches to trace amounts fell over Southwest and West Central Ne.  The wind combined with temperatures from 5 above to 5 below zero produced wind chills from 45 to 65 below zero and reduced visibilities to near zero on the 17th thru the 18th.  A portion of Interstaste 80 from Ogallala to York was closed from the night of the 17th thru the morning of the 19th. A 52 year old man was found frozen to death in his garage in Arnold Ne., Custer County on the 18th. Power lines whipped by the high winds caused 200k damage to a power substation in Broken Bow, Custer Co.  In Imperial, Chase County, three 21,000 gal fiberglass fertilizer storage tanks were blown over onto a pickup casusing extensive damage to the pickup and the 3 tanks.

Strong winds, snow and cold temperatures combined to produce blizzard conditions. High winds gusting to 65 mph resulted in a gaping 32x40 foot hole in a chicken house roof and the loss of 300 chickens north of David City.

In two separate incidents, two men suffered from frostbite after their cars stalled.  An 80 year old man from Battlecreek was traveling home when rain changed to snow and froze his accelerator pedal.  After trying to flee his vehicle, he turned back and spent the night in his truck.  A 46 year old Tilden man slid off the road and with a frozen ignition was unable to run his heater.  Bundled up in winter clothes and a blanket he spent the night in his car, but suffered frostbite to his feet.  Both men were hospitalized.
A tornado briefly touched down on Hwy 50 in Cass County, flipping a truck and camper into a ditch.

A general 5 to 6 inch band of snow fell from Lincoln and Auburn northeast to Omaha and Tekamah.  Heavier amounts of 7 to 10 inches were reported in Papillion and continued into western Iowa.  In addition to the snow, strong winds from 30 to 40 mph reduced visibilities to less than one mile for over 12 hours in Lincoln and Omaha areas.  Wind chills ranged from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  Numerous schools were closed and several businesses closed by late afternoon.

Arctic high pressure dominated the weather over much of the central United States.  Sub-zero temperatures were reported at Omaha for 63 consecutive hours from early February 1st through February 4th.  While area hospitals reported treating several people for frostbite, one frostbite victim was detailed in a newspaper article.  The peak of the cold wave occurred during the morning of February 2nd with a low temperature of 23 degrees below zero at Omaha.  Many locations reported lows from 20 to 25 degrees below zero that morning.

Heavy rainfall throughout the month resulted in flash flooding along the Little Nemaha River in Otoe County on May 9th, the 23rd, and the 27th.  Damage estimates to public structures amounted to $618,000 and nearly $6 million to crops.

Heavy rains resulted in flash flooding and flooding along the Little Nemaha River and across Nemaha County from the 9th through the 12th, the 23rd, and the 25th through the 31st.  Damage to bridges and culverts was estimated at $682,000 and damage to crops was $2.1 million.

Heavy rains of 15 to 18 inches saturated soils throughout the month of May in Johnson County and resulted in flash flooding on the 23rd and the 26th.  Damage to bridges and culverts was estimated at $404,000 and crop damage was estimated at $1.58 million.
Heavy rains throughout the month saturated soils across Cass County and resulted in flash flooding on the 23rd.  Damage to crops was estimated at nearly $55,000.

Heavy rains throughout the month of May saturated soils and produced flooding along the Big Blue River and the Lincoln Creek from May 27th through the 28th.  Damage to roads and ditches was estimated at $100,000.

A blast of Arctic air invaded eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 17th.  The day started out with dense fog and temperatures around freezing.  By late afternoon, a large temperature gradient had been established as Norfolk had switched over to freezing drizzle and Falls City was in the 60's.  A strong cold front moved through the area plummeting temperatures and was accompanied by sustained winds from 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  While light rain preceded the frontal passage, only a dusting to 1 inch of snow fell behind the cold front.  Extreme wind chills from -40 to -60 degrees persisted for 31 hours.

While most areas received 3 to 5 inches of new snow, parts of Brown County reported 6 to 8 inches.  The new snow and northwest winds gusting to 60 mph created near-zero visibilities and snow drifts up to 8 feet.  Highways and local roads were impassable.  The strong winds and single digit temperatures combined to produce extreme wind chills of 45 to 60 degrees below zero.  All schools and most businesses were closed the 18th.  Numerous local power outages occurred; lasting an average of 2 to 3 hours.  Only minor livestock losses were reported, which included a few hogs in Holt County.

A major winter storm with sustained winds of 40 mph and gusts to 65 mph dropped 5 to 6 inches of snow over Sheridan, Cherry, Grant, Thomas and Custer counties of North Central Ne.  Three inches to trace amounts fell over Southwest and West Central Ne.  The wind combined with temperatures from 5 above to 5 below zero produced wind chills from 45 to 65 below zero and reduced visibilities to near zero on the 17th thru the 18th.  A portion of Interstaste 80 from Ogallala to York was closed from the night of the 17th thru the morning of the 19th. A 52 year old man was found frozen to death in his garage in Arnold Ne., Custer County on the 18th. Power lines whipped by the high winds caused 200k damage to a power substation in Broken Bow, Custer Co.  In Imperial, Chase County, three 21,000 gal fiberglass fertilizer storage tanks were blown over onto a pickup casusing extensive damage to the pickup and the 3 tanks.

An intense spring storm system brought beneficial rains to the area, but also a blizzard.  During the afternoon and evening of the 24th, rain changed to freezing rain and then snow.  Snowfall from 2 to 8 inches fell, with the heavier, 6 inch or greater band from near Grand Island northeast to Norfolk and Laurel.  Winds were sustained at 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero from 8 pm the 24th until 6 am the 25th.  Temperatures fell to the single digits with wind chills ranging from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  This dangerous storm stranded hundreds of motorists and resulted in numerous accidents and reports of wind damage.

Lightning struck a garage and traveled down a metal brace for the garage door.  A man sitting on a metal bucket at the time was leaning against the brace.  The bolt entered his body through his back.  The force of this threw him forward into the garage and onto the floor.  He was hospitalized and spent the night in the ICU and then was released the next day.
Heavy rains of 2 to 4 inches resulted in the Sand Creek coming out of its bank.

High based funnel on the north side of the main thunderstorm cell.  Seen and videotaped by storm chaser.
A tornado briefly touched down in southwest Knox County.

A tornado made brief touchdowns between Orchard and just east of Royal.  The tornado did partial damage to three farmsteads, flattened trees and powerlines, and upended irrigation systems in its path.

Thunderstorms developed near Holdrege and moved eastward across the area.  The storms produced mainly very strong winds to near 90 mph and hail up to 2 inches in diameter.  One tornado briefly touched down near Naponee in open country.  A house near Cairo had its front porch ripped off by very strong winds.  Crops in the Holdrege area sustained considerable damage.
A complex of severe thunderstorms moved into western Buffalo county bringing hail to size of tennis balls.  Pockets of minor crop damage resulted.
Strong thunderstorm winds blew over a semi tractor-trailernear Potter.
A tornado briefly touched down in an open field nine miles northeast of Scottsbluff.
Large hail did considerable damage to thousands of acres of crops from Hooper to Fremont.  Estimates of 4,000 homes and countless vehicles had damage from the winds and hail.

A tornado touched down in the Fremont area and did the most significant damage in the Fremont Lakes recreation area.  While sending 30 campers to a brick shelter, a tornado did damage to 5 camping trailers and resulted in several downed trees.
Lightning struck the corner of a machine shed near an electrical box.  The resulting fire destroyed the shed and all its contents.A car parked next to the building was also destroyed.
A dozen farmsteads suffered damage as a tornado touched down south of Snyder and west of Scribner.  The most significant damage was 5.5 miles west of Scribner, where six farm buildings were lost.
A tornado touched down and took the roof off the Abie Auditorium.  At least 10 farm structures were damaged and 5 center pivot irrigation systems were damaged.
Large hail in Elkhorn resulted in broken car windows.
A brief tornado touchdown did some roof damage in rural Loma.

The beginning of the tornado started 4 1/2 miles west northwest of downtown Beatrice, just north of the Homestead National Monument.  The storm damaged trees just north of state highway 4 when it struck several homes, primarily lifting the roofs off of them.  The tornado was rated F1 at that point.  Around 1/2 mile to the east, the tornado struck a subdivision.  The damage was more severe, with collapsed outer walls and roofs off homes.  The tornado damage was rated as a strong F2 in this area.  The tornado, continuing to move in an east southeast direction, nearly paralleling highway 4, struck a church.  Part of the roof was lost off the church.  After striking the church, the tornado hit several more homes and barns before entering the heart of the city.  The damage path width of the tornado was 1/4 to 1/2 mile wide, with damaging thunderstorms winds out to 11/2 mile.  The main tornado path remained 1/4 to 1/2 mile wide north of U.S. highway 136, with much of the damage south of highway 136 due to thunderstorm downburst winds.  The tornado strength weakened when entering the city, with an F-scale strength between F0-F1.  Although much of the damage in the central city area was due to falling trees and large tree limbs, other damage noted was roofing torn off of several buildings and a collapsed 200 foot communication tower.  After the storm exited the city, it regained strength.  The tornado also began curving to the northeast.  The storm maintained about F1 strength from about 1 mile east of downtown Beatrice to the storm's dissipation, approximately 3 miles northeast of the city.  The strong F2 tornado's path was 9.5 miles long.
Winds gusting to 80 mph downed six large trees at the Coleridge Cemetary and toppled trees throughout the city.

Downburst winds on the back side of a severe thunderstorm caused  tree and property damage in Edison.  Two mobile homes suffered damage, a semi tanker was overturned and an electrical substation was completely destroyed.  Considerable crop damage was incurred from the high wind and small hail in the Edison area.

Thunderstorms moved south into Buffalo county dropping hail up to the size of baseballs on the east side of Kearney.  The storms weakened some after moving south of Kearney, but still managed to produce 1.00 diameter hail in Franklin before dissipating.
A wind gust overturned a semi-trailer at Hwy 77 and Interstate 80.
A tornado touched down in the Beemer area, damaging two homes and a car in Beemer, flattening buildings, and downing several trees.  The roof was also taken off a 100 foot long cattle shed.
A tornado was reported by spotters.  A large grain bin was blown over and power lines were downed.
Baseball sized hail did signficant damage to crops.
CENTER PIVOT IRRIGATION SYSTEM AND TWO GRAIN BINS DESTROYED. APPROXIMATELY 30,000 ACRES OF CORN DAMAGED.
TWO PUBLIC REPORTS OF BRIEF TORNADO TOUCHDOWN NEAR NORTH SHORE OF MERRITT RESERVOIR.
Lightning struck Gretna City Hall and did damage to phones and computers.
Lightning destroyed a barn and its contents.
Heavy rains from 6 to 10 inches fell over northern Cedar County.  The flooding lead to three dozen bridges being washed away, and two to three dozen roads being closed.
Several trees were downed in Ralston due to thunderstorm winds.
Locally 5.5 inches of rain fell in southeast Platte County in a short 11/2 hour period.  Numerous fields flooded with damage estimated at 2 million dollars.
Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms in and near Kimball causedsome some urban flooding.
LAW ENFORCEMENT REPORTED A BRIEF TORNADO TOUCHDOWN.
Thunderstorms producing large hail resulted in over 1,000 claims of damage to vehicles and homes in the Plattsmouth area.
Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage and also damaged power lines and some buildings.
Large hail caused widespread crop damage.
Thunderstorm winds took the roof off a maintenance building.  Several trees were downed.
A supercell thunderstorm moved southeast across eastern Keith county destroying crops in a 6 mile wide 18 mile long path.
Brief tornado touchdown reported near highway 81 and highway 98.  Damage was done to a garage.
Lightning damaged an electrical substation and caused a power outage over much of South Sioux City.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree and power line damage, destroyed farm buildings, and caused scattered damage to homes. The thunderstorm winds affected most of Dakota County.

Late night severe thunderstorms moved east from Hall in the Hamilton and Merrick counties leaving large hail and producing winds gusts near 60 mph.  A lightning strike near a mobile home in Aurora caused a small electrical fire resulting in minor damage to the home.

After two days of rain, southern Lancaster County received between 6 and 9 inches of rain.  The copius amounts of rain resulted in flashing flooding on the Salt Creek at Roca, Lincoln, and Greenwood from late Wednesday, May 8th, to early May 9th.  Damage estimates due to flooding was estimated at $1.5 million.
TREE BRANCHES DOWN.
TREE BRANCHES 2 INCHES IN DIAMETER DOWN.
Heavy rainfall caused two to three inches of water runningover rural roads in southwest Kimball County.
LARGE FUEL TANKS BLOWN OVER. LARGE TREE LIMBS DOWN.
A severe thunderstorm moved across Harlan and Franklin Counties producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter.
A supercell thunderstorm moved southeast across northern frontier county causing extensive crop damage.  The storm blew over grain bins, destroyed a greenhouse, blew down trees and destroyed crops.
Hail caused considerable crop damage in an area from north of Bushnell to 15 miles northwest of Potter.  The swath of hail was 6 miles wide.
Hail further damaged crops in an area from just east of Potter to near the Cheyenne county line.
Thunderstorm winds damaged the roof of the Co-op in Richland, a camper, outbuildings, and several businesses in Schuyler.  Several signs and windows were broken.

A tornado set down 5 miles southwest of Gilead and traveled northeast.  The tornado totally destroyed the St. Paul Lutheran Church and did extensive damage to the Parsonage.  The roof of the church ended up entering the kitchen of the Parsonage.  The tornado also did considerable damage to 3 other farmsteads along its path.

A thunderstorm developed north of Hastings and moved east.  It dropped upto golfball size hail around Hastings.  Very heavy rains fell in an area from near Hastings to Geneva.  Especially hard hit was Fillmore County where several inches of rain fell.  Portions of US Highway 81 and Nebraska Highway 41 were underwater for a time.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees 2.5 miles north of Valparaiso.
Lightning damaged the computer and phone systems at the Milford Elementary School.
Hail, up to one and three quarters of an inch fell in a pathfrom 5 miles south of Dix to 2 miles south of Dix.

Severe thunderstorms developed along US Highway 81 between York and the Nebraska-Kansas state line south of Hebron.  These storms moved east and dropped mainly 3/4 inch hail but some stones that fell south of Hebron did reach golfball size.

Thunderstorm winds caused widespread damage over most of Dakota county. The winds caused widespread tree damage, blew down power lines and poles, destroyed many farm buildings, and damaged many homes. Livestock loss resulted from some of the destroyed farm buildings. Crops were also damaged, although most of the crop damage was attributed to accompanying large hail.

Large hail caused devastating crop damage in much of Dakota County with many crops totally destroyed. Very strong winds and heavy rain contributed to the damage. An estimated half of the county's 64,300 acres of corn was wiped out with about half of the remaining corn acreage sustaining damage. About a third of the county's 52,200 acres of beans were wiped out while most of the remaining bean acreage sustained varying damage. Smaller crops also suffered extensive damage. The hail also caused damage to vehicles and buildings.
Thunderstorms produced rainfalls of 2 to 3 inches causing some flooding to occur.  A bridge over Turkey Creek, northeast of Arapahoe, collapsed.
Lightning struck the St. Johns Catholic Church and started it on fire.  The resulting fire completely destroyed the building.
Several funnel clouds were observed near Potter.

Severe thunderstorms developed west of Grand Island and Hastings and moved east.  They produced winds to 80 miles an hour and hail up to golfball size.  Most damage was to trees, especially in Hamilton, York and Polk Counties.
Strong winds caused tree damage in McCool Junction.  Several limbs up to 8 inches in diameter fell due to the wind.
Rains of 1 to 3 inches fell over already saturated ground.
Rains of 1 to 3 inches fell over already saturated ground.
Elgin reported flash flooding with locally heavy rain.
Rains of 1 to 3 inches fell over already saturated ground resulting in flash flooding.
F0 tornado touched down briefly destroying a calving shed.
Brief tornado touchdown reported near Plainview with severe damage to a farm.
A tornado damaged a grain bin, barn, uprooted trees, and a tree fell on a house.
An area three to four miles wide and nine miles long received hail damage, some of which was a loss of 100%.
Lightning struck a large Linden tree, splitting it to its base and sending branches through a bedroom window.
Lightning started a fire in the attic and damaged the wiring.  Damage due to fire and smoke was $12,000.
A tornado caused some broken windows.

Large hail and frequent lightning occurred from four miles westof Alliance to Alliance.  In that area, many cars were damaged.     House windows were broken and some roofs were damaged as well.  Lightning caused power to be out for an hour in about one third of Alliance.  Lightning also struck a few trees, causing some damage.
Severe thunderstorms developed west of York and moved east dropping upto golfball size hail.
A severe thunderstorm developed northwest of Beaver City and moved southeast.  Large hail was driven by strong winds that destroyed most wheat fields in its path.
PUBLIC REPORTED BRIEF TORNADO TOUCHDOWN 17 NW NORTH PLATTE.
WINDOWS ON NORTH SIDE OF HOUSE BLOWN OUT. MANY TREE LIMBS DOWN.
EXTENSIVE TREE DAMAGE ACROSS THE CITY.

EXTENSIVE TREE DAMAGE.
Between 330PM and 400PM, three quarter inch to one and one half inch diameter hail fell in the Harrisburg area.
TREES BLOWN DOWN. WINDOWS IN HOUSE AND PICKUP BROKEN. ROOF AND SIDING DAMAGE.
Significant crop damage was reported from Osmond to northeast of Pierce due to large hail.

Morning thunderstorms moved across southern Nebraska resulting in some property and crop damage.  Damage to the computer room was incurred at the Gothenburg High School.  A grain bin was moved from its base north of Inavale and a portion of a roof torn off from a business in downtown Red Cloud.
Heavy rains fell in a 15 mile wide band over southern Lancaster, northern Johnson, northern Nemaha, and southern Otoe Counties.
TORNADO ON THE GROUND FOR 3 MILES. THREE FARMSTEADS HEAVILY DAMAGED. THREE HEAD OF LIVESTOCK KILLED. HEAVY DAMAGE TO LARGE POWER TRANSMISSION LINE. CENTER PIVOT IRRIGAION SYSTEM DESTROYED.
Lightning strikes northwest of Beatrice caused the city's water wells to shut down and took out power.  Lightning also struck a home in Beatrice doing damage to the house.
Heavy rains continued to fall over southern Lancaster during the evening accumulating to 6 to 9 inches in a narrow band.
Heavy rains of 2 to 4 inches fell over the Little and Big Blue River basins.
Heavy rains of 2 to 4 inches fell over the Little and Big Blue River basins resulting in flash flooding.
Heavy rains of 2 to 4 inches fell over Pawnee County with flooding on the Turkey Creek.
SEVERAL LARGE TREE BRANCHS DOWN
LANDSPOUT-TYPE VORTEX ON THE GROUND FOR 3 MINUTES OVER OPEN RANGELAND.
Heavy rains fell in a 15 mile wide band over southern Lancaster, northern Johnson, northern Nemaha, and southern Otoe Counties.
Heavy rain fell in a 15 mile wide band over southern Lancaster, northern Johnson, northern Nemaha, and southern Otoe Counties.

An isolated severe thunderstorm developed over parts of Douglas and Sarpy Counties.  Nearly $300,000 damage was done by hail to aircraft and vehicles at Offutt Air Force Base.  3,000 to 5,000 acres of corn and soybeans were also lost.  Numerous reports of damage to cars, homes, and businesses.
A tornado briefly touched down near Rogers.
A tornado touched down north of Arlington and damaged St. Paul's Lutheran Church and School and eight nearby farmsteads.
Above normal rainfall in the month of may sent pine creek out of its banks causing minor low land flooding.
Hail broke windows in and near Melbeda.
Lightning struck a house on North Main street, putting a hole in it about the size of a baseball.
Tornado destroyed a barn and several out buildings.  Damage wasalso down to a windbreak, seven corrals and a shed.
An irrigation pivot northwest of Alliance was thrown more than 20 feet by strong winds.
Heavy rains from 3 to 6 inches from the 19th through the 23rd, resulted in 19,000 acres of land being flooded.  Agricultural damage was estimated at 3.6 million.
Brief tornado touchdown reported at Prague.
POWER LINES DOWN
Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Snyder.
Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree and power line damage, with scattered damage to buildings also reported.
LARGE TREE LIMBS BLOWN DOWN.
CENTER PIVOT IRRIGATION SYSTEM BLOWN OVER.
Heavy rains of 3 to 6 inches from the 19th through the 23rd, resulted in flooding of the Wahoo Creek at Ithaca.  Flooding damage to roads was estimated at $30,000.
SEVERAL LARGE TREE LIMBS DOWN
EXTENSIVE CROP DAMAGE FROM WIND DRIVEN HAIL.
Six cows feeding at a metal feeder were found dead after being struck by lightning.
Lightning started a fire damaging a house an its contents.
Wind driven tennis ball sized hail resulted in severe roof, siding, and window damage in Wymore.
Lightning damaged an outbuilding near Seward.

Heavy rains of 6 to 10 inches caused flash flooding of roads and lowlands with no travel advised by local authorities. Several motorists were stranded and had to be rescued during the early morning hours. Basement flooding was widespread. Crop damage was suspected but could not be separated from damage caused by widespread hail from the same storm.
Thunderstorms developed near Kearney and moved east, dropping very heavy rains along with some golfball size hail near Doniphan.  Several streets were flooded in the Kearney area.
POWER LINES BLOWN DOWN
Hail broke a few windows.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.
A tornado destroyed farm buildings.

WHEAT FIELDS AND CORN FIELDS ACROSS PERKINS COUNTY WERE DESTROYED  BY A SERIES OF STORMS WHICH PASSED OVER THE COUNTY THE NIGHT OF JUNE 5 - JUNE 6.  SEVERAL MILLION DOLLARS IN CROP DAMAGE WAS DONE BY A COMBINATION OF LARGE HAIL AND HIGH WINDS.
DOWNBURST WINDS DAMAGED THREE GRAINBINS.  WIND DRIVEN HAIL UP TO BASEBALL SIZE CAUSED EXTENSIVE DAMAGE TO FARM BUILDINGS.
Strong winds peeled off part of a business building's roof in Snyder.

Heavy rain of 6 to 10 inches caused flash flooding of lowlands and creeks in northern and eastern Dakota County. Roads were closed by the flooding with damage occurring to some roads. Several motorists were stranded and had to be rescued during the early morning hours. Basement flooding also was widespread with resulting damage. Flooding of cropland may have damaged crops, but any damage caused by flooding could not be separated from extensive hail damage which occurred with the storm.
Large hail did considerable damage to 45,000 acres of crops from Hooper to Fremont.
HAIL SEVERAL INCHES DEEP. LARGE TREES BLOWN DOWN.
A 79 year old man was struck by lightning while watching thunderstorms.

A tornado that touched down near Gilead in Thayer County continued east into Jefferson County.  The tornado destroyed or damaged parts of four farmsteads in its path from 21/2 miles southwest of Gladstone to 3 miles southeast of Alexandria.  At least six cattle were also killed.  Total damage was estimated at $250,000.

Another tornado formed from the same supercell that produced the Gilead and Gladstone/Alexandria tornadoes.  During its 21/2 mile path, the tornado severely damaged two farmsteads and extensive damage to seven other farmsteads.  Damage was estimated at $350,000.
Thunderstorm winds downed large tree limbs.

Hail covered the ground for a depth of two inches.  Wind driven hail resulted in 2 million dollars worth of damage to soybeans, alfalfa, oats, and corn.  The path was four miles long and two miles wide, just east of Creighton.

Winds gusting to 92 miles per hour were measured at the Omaha/Valley National Weather Service.  Ten power poles snapped in front of the office, the roof was torn off a Valley convenience store,  damage was done to several center-pivot irrigation systems, and there were trees uprooted in a 1 mile radius of the National Weather Service Office.

Winds gusting to 80 mph lifted a 30 foot by 40 foot roof off a utility shed on a farmstead in Wann.  In addition to tree damage, corn cribs on another farmstead were moved 10 feet off their foundation.
Winds gusting to 80 mph resulted in downed power lines, numerous tree limbs down, and damage to the roof and garage of a home.
A thunderstorm developed near Grand Island and moved eastward.  It reached severe category near York and produced hail upto the size of golfballs.
TORNADO INTERMITTENTLY ON THE GROUND FOR APPROXIMATELY 3 MILES WITH MINOR DAMAGE TO TWO FARMS AND HEAVY DAMAGE TO ANOTHER.
LARGE HAIL DESTROYED CROPS OVER KEITH COUNTY.
MACRO/MICROBURST PRODUCED WINDS TO 75 MPH IN GRANT WITH TEMPERATURES CLIMBING FROM THE 60S TO THE LOW 80S IN A MATTER OF MINUTES.
Hail from a severe thunderstorm totally destroyed several thousand acres of standing wheat and damaged 3000 more acres.
Thunderstorm winds took the roof off a machine shed and damaged other sheds.
A tornado did damage to outbuildings and a barn 15 miles southeast of Stanton.  Spotters were able to see the funnel for several minutes.
Lightning destroyed a landmark church in Goehner.
HAIL AND WINDS UP TO 60 MPH DAMAGED WINDSHIELDS, WINDOWS, ROOFS, AND CROPS.
HAIL WIPED OUT WHEAT CROP.
Heavy rain fell over much of eastern Knox County, with water reported over the roads near Creighton.
Heavy rains of 2 to 6 inches fell over Colfax, Dodge, and southern Cuming Counties.
Heavy rains of 2 to 6 inches fell over Colfax, Dodge, and southern Cuming Counties.
Heavy rains of 2 to 6 inches fell over Colfax, Dodge, and southern Cuming Counties.
Heavy rains of 3 to 6 inches fell over Saunders County.
Hail damaged cars.
Heavy rains of 3 to 6 inches fell with flooding reported near Otavia.
Rains of 1 to 2 inches fell over already saturated grounds in Saunders County, where streams and rivers were running high.
Rains of 2 to 3 inches fell over much of Washington County, pushing the Bell and Big Papio Creeks out of banks.
A severe thunderstorm dropping hail the size of golfballs and grapefruits moved across southwest Chase County, damaging homes, out buildings, farm equipment, pivot systems and crops.
POWER LINES DOWN. LARGE TREE LIMBS DOWN. WINDOWS BROKEN OUT.
LARGE TREE LIMBS DOWN.

An upper level disturbance moved east northeast from the southern and central Rockies into the central plains as low pressure strengthened over eastern Colorado.  Freezing rain was widespread across the area causing several telephone outages, power outages,  and auto accidents.  Nearly 40,000 Omaha Public Power District (OPPD) customers in west Omaha lost power for a period of time from 11/14/96 during the early evening hours until 11/15/96 during the morning hours.

Very heavy rainfall from thunderstorms caused flooding in andaround the Sidney area.  Many roads were under water and thecity of Sidney was closed to all traffic for a time between 600PM and 1000PM.  Estimates of around 5 inches of rain were reported.  Many basements were flooded.

Winter storm began approximately 4 PM CST on the 14th with freezing drizzle causing numerous accidents across the area.  Freezing drizzle changed to snow on the 15th with amounts ranging from 3 inches at North Platte to as much as 9 inches around O neill.  Fifty power poles were snapped off in the O neill area.  Power outages were common across the area.

A strong low pressure system which moved northeast across the state into the eastern Dakotas caused strong northwesterly winds which gusted as high as 67 mph across the area.  This caused numerous power outages along with tree damage.  In Douglas County, a tree fell and destroyed a shed.  Also two trees fell on cars.  One of which was damaged significantly.  In Madison County, a large tree limb fell on a car in downtown Norfolk.

An intense spring storm system brought near blizzard conditions to the area. Snow was widespread across the area with accumulations of 2 to 5 inches common.  Strong gusty winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts into the 50s blew and drifted snow impeding travel across the area.  A family traveling on Interstate 80 in Lincoln county pulled off to the side of the Interstate due to reduced visibilities and were struck from behind by a Semi tractor-trailer and killed.  The driver of the Semi was unable to see do to blowing snow.

Strong low pressure in northwest Kansas produced very strong south winds all day in the south central Nebraska. Sustained windspeeds of 30 to 45 mph were noted with freqent gusts around 60 mph. Two grain bins near Overton were damaged. Small outbuildings and roofs were damaged in Cairo. Unharvested corn incurred minor stalk breakage due to the wind.

A strong pressure gradient behind low pressure which moved across western Nebraska into the eastern Dakotas created strong south southwesterly winds during the day.  The winds gusted as high as 62 mph across the area causing scattered power outages along with tree damage.  In Lancaster County, siding was stripped from Memorial Stadium, a tree fell on a home causing major damage, and a tree fell on two cars creating minor damage.  In Knox County, high winds near Sparta caused 14 hog farm shelters to loose their roofs, and a quonset 14 miles northeast of Verdigre was picked up and tossed into the trees.  In Cuming County, part of a roof blew off a home near Wisner.  In Stanton County, a large metal shed in Pilger was destroyed.

High winds sustained from 20 to 40 mph with gusts to 70 mph were found over Eastern Nebraska from early the 25th until early the 26th.  Winds gusting to 51 mph toppled a 65 foot tree, killing a 13 year old Omaha boy as he played outside.  A Creighton man was injured when a beam from a parking canopy was blown on him.  Knox County had several reports of windows broken and vehicle doors being blown open and breaking their springs.  At Verdigre in Knox County, high winds blew over a semi-trailer.

Deep low pressure moving east across Kansas combined with Arctic high pressure diving southeast to produce blizzard conditions over North Central Nebraska.  While most areas received 3 to 5 inches of new snow, parts of Brown County reported 6 to 8 inches.  The new snow and northwest winds gusting to 60 mph created near-zero visibilities and snow drifts up to 8 feet.  Highways and local roads were impassable.  The strong winds and single digit temperatures combined to produce extreme wind chills of 45 to 60 degrees below zero.  All schools and most businesses were closed the 18th.  Numerous local power outages occurred; lasting an average of 2 to 3 hours.  Only minor livestock losses were reported, which included a few hogs in Holt County.

A major winter storm moved through the area producing blizzard conditions.  Snowfalls averaged between 6 and 8 inches.  This snow was blown around by winds of 40 to 60 mph. A strong pressure gradient behind low pressure which moved across western Nebraska into the eastern Dakotas created strong south southwesterly winds during the day.  The winds gusted as high as 62 mph across the area causing scattered power outages along with tree damage.  In Lancaster County, siding was stripped from Memorial Stadium, a tree fell on a home causing major damage, and a tree fell on two cars creating minor damage.  In Knox County, high winds near Sparta caused 14 hog farm shelters to loose their roofs, and a quonset 14 miles northeast of Verdigre was picked up and tossed into the trees.  In Cuming County, part of a roof blew off a home near Wisner.  In Stanton County, a large metal shed in Pilger was destroyed.

Heavy rains dropped 4 to 9 inches of rain in east central Nebraska, causing record flooding to the Pebble and Maple Creeks.  Dodge reported a little more than 6 inches of rain.  Extensive damage was done to the homes and businesses in Nickerson and Winslow.  Major flooding occurred in Winslow, where all 40 homes and businesses reported flooding.  150 residents were evacuated.  County-wide, it was estimated that 100 homes were affected.  Numerous roads were closed with damage to roads and bridges around $500,000.  The cities of Nickerson and Winslow reported damage of $170,000.  Damage to 50,000 acres of crops was estimated at $2.25 million for corn and $3 million for soybeans.

An intense spring storm system brought beneficial rains to the area, but also a blizzard.  During the afternoon and evening of the 24th, rain changed to freezing rain and then snow.  Snowfall from 2 to 8 inches fell, with the heavier, 6 inch or greater band from near Grand Island northeast to Norfolk and Laurel.  Winds were sustained at 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero from 8 pm the 24th until 6 am the 25th.  Temperatures fell to the single digits with wind chills ranging from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  This dangerous storm stranded hundreds of motorists and resulted in numerous accidents and reports of wind damage.

A large severe thunderstorm moved south across a portion of south central Nebraska.  The hardest hit area was in Harlan county with significant property damage and devastating crop damage.  Hail to the size of golfballs and winds of 70 to 90 mph were reported.  Over 80,000 acres of land were damaged in a 6 mile wide corridor from north of Orleans to the Kansas border.  Rainfall in excess of five inches in 40 minutes caused significant erosion and washed out several county roads.  In Orleans, a basement wall caved in due to the heavy rain.  At least 3 grain bins were destroyed and a mobile home was moved 30 feet by the high winds.  Rural farmsteads reported downed power lines, extensive tree damage and broken windows.
Lightning struck a man who was broadcasting from a car show.  The man was sitting at a metal table with a 12-foot antenna next to him.  The man was taken to a local hospital and released.
Lightning struck a church in Clearwater.

This caused numerous power outages along with tree damage.  In Douglas County, a tree fell and destroyed a shed.  Also two trees fell on cars.  One of which was damaged significantly.  In Madison County, a large tree limb fell on a car in downtown Norfolk.

A strong cold front moved through eastern Nebraska causing precipitation to change from rain, to freezing rain, and then to snow.  The precipitation was most widespread from east of Norfolk south through Columbus, Lincoln, and Beatrice.  Trees and power lines were downed in Cuming county.  Several auto accidents were reported throughout the area as well.

An upper level disturbance moved east northeast from the southern and central Rockies into the central plains as low pressure strengthened over eastern Colorado.  Freezing rain was widespread across the area causing several telephone outages, power outages,  and auto accidents.  Nearly 40,000 Omaha Public Power District (OPPD) customers in west Omaha lost power for a period of time from 11/14/96 during the early evening hours until 11/15/96 during the morning hours.

Thunderstorm winds of 80 mph snapped off both gates of a railroad crossing, damaged fences, and damaged a couple of businesses.

An intense spring storm system brought beneficial rains to the area, but also a blizzard.  During the afternoon and evening of the 24th, rain changed to freezing rain and then snow.  Snowfall from 2 to 8 inches fell, with the heavier, 6 inch or greater band from near Grand Island northeast to Norfolk and Laurel.  Winds were sustained at 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero from 8 pm the 24th until 6 am the 25th.  Temperatures fell to the single digits with wind chills ranging from 30 to 40 degrees below zero.  This dangerous storm stranded hundreds of motorists and resulted in numerous accidents and reports of wind damage.

Snow and strong winds combined to produce blizzard conditions in the west part of the Nebraska panhandle.  Visibilities were near zero and wind chill temperatures as low as 45 degrees below zero.three to six inches of snow were common with the event.  Many roads were closed during that time.
